entry.title: Re: Best Custom Packaging Company in USA in 2025?
entry.content: Hey, great question—this is something we’ve seen more and more as packaging becomes part of the overall customer experience, not just a logistics step. From our side at SixPackRings, we work with a lot of businesses that need flexible, scalable packaging (especially for multi-packs, beverage carriers, and branded solutions). While we’re not directly embedded into SAP EWM, many of our clients integrate packaging into their workflow by treating it as a dynamic component rather than static material master data. A few things we’ve seen work well: Flexible material handling in SAP: Instead of fixed SKUs for every packaging variation, some teams use configurable materials or batch-level attributes for dimensions and branding. Middleware or APIs: Companies often use a lightweight integration layer (like REST APIs or EDI) to sync packaging specs, especially when working with external vendors. Predefined packaging logic: Setting up rules in EWM for auto-packaging based on order type, quantity, or product dimensions helps reduce manual intervention. Sustainability tracking: More clients are also tagging packaging materials with sustainability attributes (like recyclable content), which can be useful for reporting and compliance. On the packaging side, having a supplier that can adapt quickly (custom sizing, branding, eco-friendly materials) makes integration much smoother because you’re not constantly reworking your SAP setup.

entry.title: Re: SAP Build Apps Deprecation and The Path Forward
entry.content:
I understand that a product is discontinued when there isn’t enough demand for it on the market. But what’s surprising is how suddenly it’s being discontinued.
Where have all those citizen developers suddenly gone—the very people the product was aimed at?
Of course, the IT world moves very quickly, but customers don’t have the time or the budget to constantly train their teams on new tools or migrate applications.
What’s up next? The discontinuation of MDK or SAP Build Code? According to the latest usage statistics from the survey, SAP Build Code is also used very little.
SAP’s goal should actually be to quickly bring ONE technology to market that is customer-friendly in terms of pricing and feature set, and then support it for 10 years—not to develop overlapping tools in the same segment in parallel, some of which were initially unattractive to customers due to their pricing.

entry.title: Re: Standard SAP MB5S throwing TIME OUT ERROR
entry.content:
Hi,

The TIME_OUT occurs mainly due to:

1. Large data selection

MB5S reads:

Purchase orders

GR/IR balances

Material documents

FI postings

A wide date range or missing filters can easily exceed the dialog timeout limit.

2. Standard SAP timeout limit

SAP dialog work processes typically have a timeout of:

  • 600 seconds (10 mins)
  • or sometimes 1200 seconds (20 mins)

MB5S often exceeds this in dialog mode.

3. System performance or indexing issues

If indexing or buffering is slow, MB5S becomes worse.

 

Fix.

MB5S can absolutely be run in background. Many companies do this.

Steps:

  1. Go to MB5S.
  2. Enter selection parameters.
  3. Click Program → Execute in Background.
  4. Choose output (e.g., Spool (List), or XLS).
  5. Schedule immediately or periodically.

 

But this is generally not recommended, as it affects entire system dialog behavior.

Only do this if absolutely required.

 

Reduce the data volume

Use filters such as:

Company code

Purchasing organization

Plant

Smaller dataset → faster MB5S → no TIME_OUT.

entry.title: odataVersion 4.01 prevents app from being imported into Work Zone HTML5 Apps
entry.content:
Hi community,

I have a UI5 app that uses an OData service, and the odataVersion in manifest.json is set to 4.01.

      "mainService": {
        "uri": "/odata/v4/quote/",
        "type": "OData",
        "settings": {
          "annotations": [
            "annotation"
          ],
          "localUri": "localService/mainService/metadata.xml",
          "odataVersion": "4.01"
        }
      }

When I deploy this app to Cloud Foundry and refresh the Channel Manager in SAP Build Work Zone, the app fails to be imported, and the downloaded report contains the following error:

 
businessapp does not match the schema, dataPath: undefined, message: 'must be equal to one of the allowed values' [ '2.0', '4.0' ]"

Changing odataVersion to 4.0 makes the import succeed.

I believe this is a bug in Work Zone. I hope it will be fixed, but I am sharing this in case others are facing the same issue.

entry.title: Note 933420 is incorrect about ALSM_EXCEL_TO_INTERNAL_TABLE limitations
entry.content:
The note 933420 - ALSM_EXCEL_TO_INTERNAL_TABLE - SAP for Me is misleading:

  • The output structure is limited to 9999 rows and columns.

I trust the indications given in this question/answer ALSM_EXCEL_TO_INTERNAL_TABLE confusion - SAP Community:

  • nearly 70.000 rows
  • 256 columns...?

In fact, to understand why the note says "9999", or what it means, one have to look at ALSM_EXCEL_TO_INTERNAL_TABLE, it returns an internal table (parameter INTERN) with one line per value, which has this structure (ALSMEX_TABLINE):

  • ROW (4 digits)
  • COL (4 digits)
  • VALUE (50 characters)

So, ROW and COL may have a value ranging from 0 to 9999, starting from 1 for the first row.

What the note doesn't say, is that it doesn't stop returning the Excel values at the row 9999, if more lines exist in the Excel file they are returned, the ROW number is 0 for the line # 10.000, 1 for the line # 10.001 and so on.

NB: the note says that you may copy the function module to a custom one and change the type of the internal table ALSMEX_TABLINE to a custom type with the three components ROW, COL and VALUE but define them with an increased length as per your requirements.

Do you have any precise information about the real limitations other than what said here? (and possibly why it's limited to around 70000 and 256)

Thanks.

Sandra

NB: I know this function module is not supported by SAP and should not be used (it's also what the note says).

entry.title: Re: Spare Parts Discount Based on Service Contract Value...
entry.content:
Hi Adarsh,

You can update the price agreements in the service contract at the line item level for a specific spare part with 20% discount. When a service order is created in reference to the service contract, and if you include the spare part then the 20% discount will be automatically updated in the service order.

The discounts can be set at price agreements section for a specific material or group of materials using material group attribute.

Regards,

Siraj

entry.title: Re: logo condition issue in adobe forms
entry.content:
found a fix using javascript, not sure if it translates to formcalc

if ($record.HEADER.DS_EKKO.BUKRS.value != "C020") this.presence = "hidden";

a singular condition on the dark logo, which was missing the ".value" to retrieve the value of the data binding. put it on top of the white logo and now one will override the other on the layout if the condition is correct.

entry.title: Re: Supplier rebate / allowance scenario in SAP S/4HANA C...
entry.content:
Please find below answers to your questions

1) Is this the correct standard approach in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
Yes — the recommended way is to use Settlement Management with supplier condition contracts. This is the standard functionality for supplier rebates, allowances, and fixed‑amount agreements. It supports both value‑based and quantity‑based conditions, and settlement runs generate the credit notes or postings back to the supplier account.

2) Can the agreement logic be maintained by supplier + product / product group / hierarchy level?

Yes you can maintain agreements at different levels:

  • Supplier + specific material
  • Supplier + material group / hierarchy node
  • Supplier + higher product category

The condition contract allows you to define the scope (materials, groups, or hierarchies) and link it to the supplier. This way, Supplier A and Supplier B can have different rules for the same product group.

3) For fixed monthly amounts like display support, is it usually handled as part of the same supplier settlement logic, or as a separate process?
These can also be handled in Settlement Management as fixed‑amount conditions within the supplier contract. Alternatively, if the allowance is not tied to purchase volumes, some companies treat it as a periodic accrual/credit memo outside of settlement. Best practice is to keep it in the same supplier condition contract so that all supplier‑related rebates and allowances are tracked consistently.

4) If part of the agreement depends on our sales to customers instead of only purchasing-related data, is that supported in standard Public Cloud?
Supplier condition contracts are purchase‑side only. They track procurement volumes/values and fixed allowances. What is not standard is a supplier rebate that depends directly on your sell‑out data (i.e., “Supplier pays you back if you sell X units to your customers”). That cross‑link between supplier agreements and customer sales is not delivered out‑of‑the‑box in Public Cloud.

5) Tracking & Settlement
With this setup, you can clearly track:

  • What you pay each supplier (via purchase invoices).
  • What you should receive back (rebates, fixed amounts, credits).
  • How it is settled (automated settlement run generates supplier credit notes or postings).

entry.title: Re: Batch management at 'Plant level and 'Material level'...
entry.content:
1) Material‑level Batch Management (Global)
If you activate batch management at material level (OMCE), the batch is unique across all plants. Example:

  • Material ABC has batch 1000.
  • Whether you are in Plant ZXZZ or ZYZZ, batch 1000 refers to the same global batch record.

In this case, you don’t need the plant flag; the system enforces batch management everywhere automatically.

2) Plant‑level Batch Management (Local)
If you don’t activate material‑level batch management, then the plant flag (MARC‑XCHPF) becomes relevant. Example:

  • Plant ZXZZ: Material ABC has batch management active.
  • Plant ZYZZ: Material ABC does not have batch management active.

Here, batch management applies only in ZXZZ. You can create batches in ZXZZ but not in ZYZZ. This allows flexibility if some plants manage batches and others don’t.

3) Why the Plant flag is redundant when material‑level is Active
Once you switch on material‑level batch management, every plant must manage batches for that material. The plant flag then just mirrors the global setting; it doesn’t add new functionality. Example:

  • Material ABC has material‑level batch management active.
  • Even if MARC‑XCHPF is unticked in Plant ZYZZ, the system still enforces batch management globally.

So the plant flag doesn’t change behavior; it only makes the plant field visible in MSC1N. Having said that the plant flag’s purpose is to support local batch management when global (material‑level) is not enforced. Once global is active, the plant flag is effectively redundant.

entry.title: Advanced Returns Management (CBAR) and Quantity Contracts
entry.content:
Hi all, I’d appreciate your input on the following issue.

We have process where the flow is:
1. Quantity Contract > Sales Order > Delivery > Invoice (F2)
2. The customer was invoiced too high a price, so we create a CBAR with reference to the F2 document for a value correction only, using:

  • Return Reason: Customer invoiced too much
  • Follow-Up Activity: Material Remains at Customer Site
  • Refund Type: Credit Memo
  • Refund Control: Refund Allowed

3. We then create a Debit Memo Request (type DR) with reference to the F2 document for the correct price the customer actually owes.

Issue: When the sales order is created with reference to the quantity contract, the contract quantity is consumed correctly. However, once the CBAR is created with reference to the billing document, the contract quantity is reopened. This happens even though there is no physical return and the material remains at the customer site.

The later debit memo process does not consume the quantity again, so the contract open quantity becomes incorrect. From a business perspective, this is problematic because the goods were still sold; only the price was corrected.

Findings: Our current assumption is that SAP treats the CBAR as returns-relevant because item category RENV is configured as a returns item. This seems similar to SAP Note 3604002, where SAP confirms that a return order created for a sales order referencing a quantity contract reopens the contract quantity. However, it is unclear whether SAP also intends this behavior for ARM CBAR scenarios that are value-only.

Tests: Initial tests showed that the contract quantity is already affected at CBAR creation.
The SDF / free-of-charge follow-on flow does not seem to be the trigger, which aligns with SAP Note 3666149.

We also tested removing the “Sched. Line Allowed” indicator (based on SAP Note 610629), but then the quantity was no longer registered properly and a complete CBAR could not be created. The change was reverted.
This suggests that simply removing schedule line relevance is not a viable solution for RENV.

Main question: Is it standard SAP behaviour that a value-only CBAR reopens quantity contract quantities, or is this just a side effect of the returns item category/document flow design?

Open points/We are mainly trying to understand:

  • Should a value-only CBAR affect a quantity contract at all?
  • Does SAP Note 3604002 also apply to ARM/CBAR, or only to classic return orders?
  • Is there any SAP-supported way to keep ARM standard behavior, but prevent contract quantity reopening in value-only cases?
  • If not, should price corrections for quantity-contract sales be handled with a different process?

Any input would be greatly appreciated.
If you need more detailed information, I've created this blog post as well: Questions: Relationship between CBAR (Accelerated Return) and Quantity Contracts in S/4HANA

entry.title: Re: How to Implement Digital Signatures in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud (Invoices & POs)?
entry.content: For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Cloud, this is a common limitation area. DocuSign via SAP Signature Management is mainly designed for document signing workflows, not for embedding signatures directly into output forms like invoices or POs, so it’s not fully suited for print outputs. In real setups, the practical approach is: use Adobe Forms for output and handle digital signature externally, or route documents through an integration layer like SAP Business Technology Platform where signing services (DocuSign or others) can be applied before sending to customers. For Public Cloud, direct form-level digital signature embedding is limited, so most projects rely on API-based signing or external services rather than native SAP-only solutions. If your requirement is compliance-level signing on invoices, you’ll likely need BTP or middleware, otherwise standard output forms won’t fully cover it.

entry.title: Re: How to Implement Digital Signatures in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud (Invoices & POs)?
entry.content: For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Cloud this is a known limitation SAP Signature Management with DocuSign is mainly for approval workflows not for embedding signatures directly into invoice or PO outputs In real setups documents are generated using Adobe Forms and then sent through SAP Business Technology Platform or middleware to a signing service where the PDF is signed and returned Direct digital signature inside standard output forms is limited so external signing via API is the practical approach

entry.title: Re: Currency Conversion in SAP Datasphere: The Next Level
entry.content:
Thank you for the blog post! Your approach is clever and provide flexibility for the users, which can decide the currency they want to see in their reports.

I wonder how the performance is when doing this currency conversion "on the fly" in the Analytic Model, especially with models having a lot of records. Do you have any insights regarding that?

For one of our customer, we implemented something slightly different, but that provides similar functionality. In a view in our propagation layer, we created several dedicated columns for amounts in various currencies (e.g. company currency, global currency, and fixed currencies such as USD and CAD), each with their corresponding currency column. We persist that view for performance purposes (this is actually the key for top performance in our case - especially when using the partitioning feature). In a subsequent view in the reporting layer, there are two "dynamic" columns, one for the amount, and the other for the currency. These two columns contain a simple "CASE.. WHEN.. END" formula based on an input parameter that selects the amount and currency columns based on the selection of the input parameter. The input parameter is passed to the Analytic Model, and only the two "dynamic" fields are shown in the report.

It is always interesting to share (and compare!) the different approach we are implementing to resolve similar problems.

Cheers! Alex

entry.title: How to clear the freight clearing account in TM without recalculating the freight settlement?
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I’m facing an issue with clearing the freight clearing (GR/IR) account in a TM scenario.

Here is the situation:

We have one Freight Order with two Freight Settlement Documents:

  • Settlement 1: amount = 100
  • Settlement 2: amount = 200

Then, the first supplier invoice arrives with an amount of 100.
We post it via MIRO referencing Freight Settlement 1 (also 100).
Since both amounts match, the clearing account is balanced correctly.

After that, the second invoice arrives with an amount of 150.
However, Freight Settlement 2 was created with an amount of 200.
When we post the MIRO referencing Settlement 2, a difference of 50 remains in the clearing account.

My question is:

👉 How can we clear this difference using TM, without recalculating or changing the Freight Settlement Document?

Is there any standard process to handle this kind of variance directly in TM?

Thanks in advance for your help!

entry.title: SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform 2025 for Administrators
entry.content:
Hi,

SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform 4.3 reached the end of support. SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform 2025 is the new release for all users and administrators who want to implement a reporting solution for business users in a company. As a SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform 2025 administrator, you have to know how to set and how to secure and administer platform in this product. In addition, you have also to manage and operate servers in this platform.

 

1 - Learning Journey for Administrators in SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform 2025:

You can choose to train yourself by following on-line on your own our dedicated learning journey (free of charge) :

SAP Learning Journey for SAP BI 2025 administrators

 https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/securing-and-administering-the-sap-businessobjects-bi-platform?searchId=b05c8bae-5a50-4279-9ca0-77f5827188c0

 

This free SAP Learning Journey contains two on-line courses :

  • Securing Application and Content in SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform (BOE510)

SAP Learning course

https://learning.sap.com/courses/securing-application-and-content-in-sap-businessobjects-bi-platform

This free course makes you able to secure and implement security in SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform 2025. There is neither practice system nor instructor in this course.

 

  • Administrating Servers in SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform (BOE520)

SAP Learning course

 

 

 

https://learning.sap.com/courses/administrating-servers-in-sap-businessobjects-bi-platform

This free course makes you able to operate servers, services and nodes in SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform 2025. There is neither practice system nor instructor in this course.

 

2 - Instructor-Led Training for SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform 2025 :
You can also be trained in a classroom by following public courses for administrators in SAP BusinessObjects BI Platform 2025. Two public courses are available for participants (not free of charge) :

  • SAP BusinessObjects Business Intelligence Platform 2025: Securing Application and Content (BOE510)

SAP public course

https://training.sap.com/course/boe510-sap-businessobjects-business-intelligence-platform-2025-securing-application-and-content-remoteclassroom-025-fr-en?

 

This two-day course is designed to give you the skills to administer and secure the SAP BusinessObjects Business Intelligence (BI) platform 2025. You will learn how to identify its components, manage content, set up users and groups, configure security, and distribute content using scheduling and publications.

 

  • SAP BusinessObjects Business Intelligence Platform 2025: Administering Servers (BOE520)

SAP public course

https://training.sap.com/course/boe520-sap-businessobjects-business-intelligence-platform-2025-administering-servers-remoteclassroom-025-fr-en?

 

This three-day course is designed to give you the skills to administer the BI platform 2025 Servers and how they interact with each other to maintain, process, and deliver Business Intelligence content, such as Web Intelligence documents and Crystal Reports. We will explore this multi-tiered architecture deployment: Web, management, processing, and storage tiers. Extend your knowledge about key system deployment tasks, such as installation, promotion management and disaster recovery

Regards,

Pierre LANSALOT-BASOU

SAP Learning France

entry.title: How to manage documents in SAP Document AI
entry.content:
Hi, 

The tutorial https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/cp-aibus-dox-swagger-ui.html shows how to upload documents in SAP Document AI via API call. So I have following questions. 

1. If I develop a solution e.g. in SAP Build Process Automation to call SAP Document API, then there will numerous documents that will get uploaded in the document AI to extract information. After an extraction is over, the document is not used again.

Because I think we can't reuse the same document already uploaded instead of user again uploading it. So is there any best practice on how to manage the huge volume of documents after extraction is over. Should we set up another automation to delete the historical documents via calling an API?

2. In this tutorial, we are first creating a client. Is this an one time activity? Is this done to keep my test separate from others. In the productive use case, do I really need to setup client. The Document AI UI doesn't provide the option to client management.(at lease the base plan).

Thanks,
Mainak

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud for Administrators
entry.content:
Hi,

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) has been released by SAP in 2017. SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) is a Software As A Service (SaaS) public cloud product for data visualization both of your On-Premise and Cloud data sources. This cloud product is not a usual reporting tool, like SAP BusinessObjects BI 2025, for instance, even if you can display your live and your imported data in dedicated pages (stories).

Note : did you know that its former name was "SAP Business Objects Cloud" (BOC) at the very first beginning  in 2017 (quite confusing, isn't it) ?

As a SAP Analytics Cloud administrator, you have to know how to set and how to manage content and data security in this product. In addition, you have also to create and manage system data connections to make available your On-Premise and Cloud data sources for SAP Analytics Cloud stories and models.

 

 

 1 - SAP Learning Journey for Administrating SAP Analytics Cloud :

You can choose to train yourself by following on-line on your own our dedicated learning journey (free of charge) :

 

SAP Learning Journey for administrators in SAP Analytics Cloud

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journeys/administrating-sap-analytics-cloud?searchId=3be2752e-a37d-487d-8dba-1bc765cc70d3

 

This free Learning Journey contains three on-line courses :

  • Managing Security and Administration in SAP Analytics Cloud (SACA22)

SAP Learning Course

https://learning.sap.com/courses/managing-security-and-administration-in-sap-analytics-cloud

This free course makes you able to create and manage users and roles, secure content and data, transport content, configure custom and optional authentication, and monitor an SAP Analytics Cloud system. There is neither practice system nor instructor in this course.

 

  • Creating Data Connections for On-Premise Data Sources in SAP Analytics Cloud (SACA31)

SAP Learning Course

 

https://learning.sap.com/courses/creating-data-connections-for-on-premise-data-sources-in-sap-analytics-cloud

This free course makes you able to create live and import data connections to an on-premise data source in SAP Analytics Cloud. You will also see how to install and configure the SAP Analytics Cloud Connector (SCC) to make available your on-premise data source for import data connections. There is neither practice system nor instructor in this course.

 

  • Creating Data Connections for Cloud Applications in SAP Analytics Cloud (SACA32)

SAP Learning Course

https://learning.sap.com/courses/creating-data-connections-for-cloud-applications-in-sap-analytics-cloud

This free course makes you able to create live and import data connections to cloud applications in SAP Analytics Cloud. There is neither practice system nor instructor in this course.

 

2 - Instructor-Led Training for Administrating SAP Analytics Cloud :

On another hand, you can also choose to be trained in a classroom by following the public course for administrators, which is delivered by an instructor with a practice system for participants :

  • SAP Analytics Cloud: Administration and Data Connectivity (SACA21)

 https://training.sap.com/course/saca21-sap-analytics-cloud-administration-and-data-connectivity-remoteclassroom-061-fr-en?

This course provides the knowledge required to administer your SAP Analytics Cloud tenant and connect to various SAP and non-SAP data sources. It features practical methods for applying the concepts and plenty of hands-on exercises. Duration for this public course is 5 days.

Regards,
Pierre LANSALOT-BASOU
SAP instructor - SAP France

entry.title: My 26-Year BI Semantic Layer Journey: From BusinessObjects to SAP's Cloud & AI Future
entry.content:
When I first joined Business Objects back in 1998, the world of Business Intelligence was a fundamentally different landscape. Data lived on-premise. Servers hummed in corporate basements. The "cloud" was something you saw out the window, not something you deployed a data model on. Over the past 26 years, I've had a front-row seat — and often a hands-on role — in the evolution of the BI semantic layer, from its earliest incarnations as a simple metadata abstraction layer to today's AI-infused, cloud-native intelligent data platforms.

This blog is a reflection on that journey: the technologies I've worked with, the strategic pivots I've witnessed, and where SAP's current vision is taking us next.

1998–2008: The Foundation Years — UNV & BEx Queries (On-Premise)

Business Objects Universe (.UNV)

My journey began in 1998 when I joined Business Objects, a company that was arguably the pioneer of the modern BI semantic layer concept. At the heart of everything was the Universe (.UNV) — a metadata layer that abstracted the complexity of relational databases into business-friendly objects that end users could drag and drop to build queries and reports.

The Universe Designer was elegant in its purpose: it sat between the raw database schema and the business user, translating SQL joins, complex table structures, and database-specific syntax into intuitive classes, objects, and predefined filters. Business users didn't need to know SQL. They didn't need to understand star schemas or snowflake models. They simply needed to understand their business questions, and the Universe would handle the rest.

This was a purely on-premise world. The Universe connected to on-premise relational databases — Oracle, SQL Server, DB2, Teradata — and the entire BI stack, from the Connection Server to the Central Management Server (CMS) to the Web Intelligence report server, lived behind the corporate firewall. Deployment was complex. Upgrades were monumental. But the semantic layer concept was powerful, and it became the backbone of enterprise reporting for thousands of organizations worldwide.

SAP BW & BEx Queries

On the SAP side of the house, SAP Business Warehouse (BW) was the dominant analytics platform, and its semantic layer equivalent was the BEx Query. Built on top of BW InfoProviders — InfoCubes, DSOs, MultiProviders — BEx Queries defined the analytical view of data using key figures, characteristics, variables, and calculated/restricted key figures.

BEx Query Designer was the tool of choice for SAP BW developers, and BEx Analyzer (an Excel add-in) was the primary consumption tool for business users. The BEx query served as the semantic definition layer that determined what data was exposed, how it was filtered, and how it was presented.

For organizations running SAP ERP, BW with BEx was the standard analytics path. The data pipeline was clear: ERP → BW (via extractors and data flows) → BEx Query → BEx Analyzer or Web Application Designer.

Key takeaway from this era: The semantic layer was established as an essential architectural component. Whether you were in the Business Objects ecosystem or the SAP BW ecosystem, the principle was the same — abstract complexity, empower business users, govern data access. But everything was on-premise, and the two ecosystems existed largely in parallel.

2010: The Convergence — UNX, Multisource Universes & the Dawn of HANA

SAP's Acquisition of Business Objects (2007) and Its Aftermath

SAP's acquisition of Business Objects in 2007 was transformative. By 2010, we were deep into the work of integrating two powerful but distinct BI ecosystems. One of the most significant technical outcomes was the introduction of the UNX Universe — the next-generation semantic layer built in the new Information Design Tool (IDT).

UNX — The Multisource Universe

The .UNX format was more than a file format upgrade from .UNV. It represented a fundamental architectural shift. The multisource-enabled Universe introduced the concept of a Data Foundation that could federate data from multiple heterogeneous sources within a single semantic layer. You could join tables from an Oracle database with tables from a SQL Server database — or even with OLAP sources — within one Universe.

This was significant because it acknowledged an emerging reality: enterprise data was no longer confined to a single monolithic database. Organizations were beginning to experiment with cloud-based data sources, SaaS application data, and Hadoop-based data lakes. The multisource Universe, powered by the Data Federation engine, was SAP's answer to this growing complexity.

The Information Design Tool also introduced:

  • Business Layer separation from the Data Foundation, enabling cleaner semantic modeling
  • Linked Universes and shared Data Foundations for reuse and governance
  • Support for OLAP connections (including BW via BICS) alongside relational connections
  • Enhanced security modeling with business security profiles and data security profiles

The UNX Universe could now serve as a unified semantic layer across on-premise and early cloud data sources, marking the beginning of the hybrid era.

SAP HANA — Calculation Views & HANA CDS Views

2010 also marked the emergence of SAP HANA — a game-changing in-memory database and application platform that would reshape SAP's entire product strategy.

From a semantic layer perspective, HANA introduced its own modeling constructs:

  • Calculation Views — the primary HANA native semantic layer, created in HANA Studio (an Eclipse-based IDE). Calculation Views allowed developers to build complex analytical models using graphical or scripted (SQL Script) approaches, incorporating joins, unions, aggregations, calculated columns, input parameters, and hierarchies directly within the database layer.
  • HANA CDS Views — Core Data Services views that brought a higher-level, annotation-rich modeling language to HANA, enabling semantically rich data models with associations, annotations for analytics, and built-in authorization concepts.

The significance of HANA's semantic layer was profound: it pushed the semantic modeling into the database itself. Rather than having a separate metadata layer sitting on top of the database (as with UNV/UNX) or a separate query layer (as with BEx), HANA's Calculation Views meant that the database was simultaneously the computation engine and the semantic definition layer. This "code-to-data" paradigm eliminated data movement, leveraged in-memory performance, and enabled real-time analytics on transactional data.

Key takeaway from this era: The semantic layer was no longer a single-technology concept. It was becoming a multi-tier, federated architecture — Universes for cross-source abstraction, HANA Calculation Views for in-database modeling, BEx Queries for BW-based analytics — all coexisting within the SAP ecosystem. The hybrid data landscape was emerging, and SAP's semantic layer strategy was evolving to meet it.

2015–2026: The Cloud Transformation — From BW/4HANA to Business Data Cloud

This decade-plus period represents the most dramatic acceleration in the evolution of SAP's BI semantic layer and overall analytics strategy. Each year brought new platforms, new tools, and new paradigms — all moving inexorably toward the cloud.

BW/4HANA — BW Modeling Tool

SAP BW/4HANA, launched in 2016, was the cloud-optimized, HANA-native evolution of SAP BW. Gone were legacy BW objects like InfoCubes and DSOs, replaced by a simplified, HANA-optimized data model based on:

  • Advanced DSOs (aDSOs) — a single, flexible storage object
  • CompositeProviders — for virtual federation and union of data sources
  • Open ODS Views — for virtualizing external HANA tables and views
  • HANA Calculation View integration — seamless embedding of HANA native models

The BW Modeling Tool (Eclipse-based) became the primary development environment, replacing the legacy BW workbench (RSA1). BEx Queries continued to serve as the semantic consumption layer, but now they could sit on top of HANA-optimized BW models that leveraged HANA's in-memory engine for pushdown execution.

BW/4HANA represented SAP's strategy of modernizing the on-premise data warehouse while preparing customers for eventual cloud migration. It was "cloud-ready" in architecture but still deployed on-premise or in private cloud environments.

S/4HANA — Embedded Analytics & CDS Views

SAP S/4HANA brought another paradigm shift — embedding analytics directly into the transactional application. The semantic layer here was built on ABAP CDS Views with rich annotations that defined:

  • Analytical queries and cubes
  • Associations and joins
  • Authorization checks
  • UI consumption metadata (for Fiori analytical apps)

S/4HANA Embedded Analytics eliminated the traditional "extract, load, and report" pattern by enabling real-time analytical queries directly on the transactional database. The CDS view became the semantic layer for both transactional and analytical consumption, blurring the line between OLTP and OLAP in ways that were previously impossible.

For BI professionals, this meant that the semantic layer was now embedded in the application layer — a significant departure from the traditional pattern of separate BI semantic layers sitting on top of replicated data.

SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) Import & Live Connection Models

SAP Analytics Cloud represented SAP's flagship cloud-native analytics platform, and it introduced its own semantic constructs:

  • Import Models — data is ingested into SAC's in-memory store (powered by HANA Cloud under the hood). The model definition — dimensions, measures, hierarchies, formulas — is defined within SAC itself. This is a self-contained cloud semantic layer.
  • Live Connection Models — SAC connects in real-time to backend sources (BW, BW/4HANA, S/4HANA, HANA) and leverages the backend semantic layer (BEx Queries, HANA Calculation Views, CDS Views) for data definition. SAC in this mode is a visualization and planning front-end, not a semantic layer itself.

The live vs. import duality reflected the hybrid reality of SAP's customer base: many organizations still had critical data on-premise and needed real-time access, while others were ready to move data to the cloud for self-service analytics.

SAC also introduced planning models, predictive capabilities, and smart features (Smart Insights, Smart Discovery) — early signals of the AI direction that would become central to SAP's strategy.

SAP HANA Cloud — Business Application Studio

As HANA moved to the cloud, so did its semantic modeling capabilities. SAP HANA Cloud brought Calculation Views to a cloud-native deployment, and the development environment shifted from HANA Studio to SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) — a cloud-based IDE built on Eclipse Theia.

Developing Calculation Views in BAS meant that the entire development lifecycle — modeling, testing, deploying — could happen in the cloud. This was significant for organizations adopting SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) as their innovation platform, enabling cloud-native data modeling alongside application development, integration, and AI/ML services.

HANA Cloud also introduced enhanced data federation and replication capabilities, allowing Calculation Views to virtualize data from on-premise HANA, third-party databases, and cloud data sources — extending the semantic layer across hybrid landscapes.

SAP Datasphere — Graphical Views & the Business Semantic Layer

SAP Datasphere (formerly SAP Data Warehouse Cloud) represents perhaps the most significant evolution of the BI semantic layer in SAP's cloud portfolio. Datasphere was purpose-built to be the unified data integration and semantic layer for SAP's cloud analytics strategy.

Key semantic layer capabilities include:

  • Graphical Views — a visual, no-code/low-code modeling environment for building data transformations, joins, unions, calculated columns, and aggregations. Graphical Views are accessible to a broader audience than traditional Calculation Views, democratizing semantic modeling.
  • Analytical Datasets and Dimensions — purpose-built semantic types that define how data is exposed for analytical consumption
  • Business Layer — a business-friendly semantic abstraction that maps technical data models to business terminology, complete with associations, hierarchies, and authorization
  • Spaces — governance constructs that manage data access, resource allocation, and collaboration boundaries

Datasphere's semantic layer is designed to federate and harmonize data from across the hybrid landscape — SAP systems (S/4HANA, BW, SuccessFactors, Ariba), non-SAP databases, cloud data lakes, and third-party applications. It serves as the single point of semantic truth for SAP Analytics Cloud and other consumption tools.

The integration between Datasphere and SAC is particularly tight: Datasphere models are directly consumable in SAC via live connections, creating a seamless model → visualize → plan → act workflow.

SAP Databricks — Custom AI/ML

SAP's partnership with Databricks represents the convergence of BI and AI/ML. Through this collaboration, organizations can:

  • Leverage Databricks' Lakehouse architecture for large-scale data processing
  • Build custom AI and Machine Learning models using Databricks' ML runtime, MLflow, and collaborative notebooks
  • Integrate Databricks-processed data and ML model outputs back into SAP Datasphere and SAC for business consumption

This is where the semantic layer extends beyond traditional BI. AI/ML models require their own "semantic understanding" of data — feature stores, training datasets, inference pipelines — and the integration between SAP's data platforms and Databricks creates a bridge between descriptive/diagnostic analytics (traditional BI) and predictive/prescriptive analytics (AI/ML).

Business Data Cloud — Business Products & Intelligent Applications

The latest evolution — SAP Business Data Cloud — represents the culmination of SAP's semantic layer journey. Business Data Cloud is the integrated data foundation that brings together:

  • SAP Datasphere for data integration, modeling, and semantic governance
  • SAP Databricks for advanced analytics, AI/ML, and data engineering
  • Business Content and Business Products — prebuilt, SAP-curated data models and semantic definitions aligned to SAP business processes (procurement, finance, supply chain, HR)

Intelligent Applications built on Business Data Cloud leverage the semantic layer not just for human consumption (dashboards, reports) but for AI agent consumption — AI systems that can understand business context, query data semantically, and take automated actions.

This is the semantic layer's ultimate evolution: from human abstraction layer to AI abstraction layer. The same semantic definitions that help a business user understand revenue by region now help an AI agent understand what "revenue by region" means in context, how to query it, and what actions to recommend based on the results.

SAP's Strategic Direction: Business Suite in the Cloud & AI First

Across all these technologies, SAP's strategic direction is clear and focused:

Priority remains Business Suite in the Cloud and AI First.

This means:

  1. Cloud-native is the primary investment vector. Datasphere, SAC, HANA Cloud, Business Data Cloud, and Databricks integration represent the future of SAP analytics and data management.
  2. AI is embedded, not bolted on. From SAP Joule (the AI copilot) to predictive planning in SAC to custom ML in Databricks, AI is becoming a first-class citizen across the analytics stack.
  3. Hybrid scenarios are supported and respected. SAP recognizes that essential on-premise data management layers — BW/4HANA, S/4HANA, even legacy BW — will continue to operate for years to come. The cloud-native applications are designed to coexist with and enhance on-premise systems, not immediately replace them. Live connections, data replication, federation, and integration services ensure that on-premise data is accessible and valuable within the cloud analytics ecosystem.

Our Commitment to Education and Enablement

As someone who has spent 26 years in this space — living through every platform shift, every tool migration, every strategic pivot — I understand deeply that technology transitions are also people transitions. New platforms are only as powerful as the people who can use them effectively.

Our education priority will align with SAP's BI strategy of Business Suite in the Cloud with an AI focus:

  • For cloud and AI: We are investing heavily in training content for Datasphere, SAC, HANA Cloud, Business Data Cloud, Databricks integration, and AI/ML capabilities. This is where the future is, and this is where our education roadmap is concentrated.
  • For customers who still need to support on-premise solutions like BusinessObjects 2025, we remain committed to your success. We will continue to offer free e-learning learning journeys through learning.sap.com, covering Universe design (UNX), Web Intelligence, Crystal Reports, and other on-premise BI tools. Free training will continue to be offered via learning.sap.com with the support of learning groups — communities of practice where learners can collaborate, ask questions, and share knowledge.
  • For deeper enablement, our paid subscription to the SAP Learning Hub provides access to expert-led live sessions and practice systems — invaluable resources for maintaining mission-critical BI on-premise solutions while gradually building competency in cloud and AI technologies. These resources are designed to help existing customers bridge the gap between their current on-premise reality and their cloud and AI future.

Looking Ahead: 2026 and Beyond

The future is strong and focused on SAP's Cloud and AI strategy. I look forward to our team's collaboration as we navigate this next chapter together.

In 2025 and 2026, we are embracing AI not only as a subject we teach but as a tool that transforms how we teach:

  • AI-Assisted Certification — the Get Certified program is being rolled out in 2025, leveraging AI to create more adaptive, personalized, and rigorous certification experiences.
  • AI-Enhanced Content Development — we are exploring and implementing newer ways of developing educational content using AI, from intelligent content generation to adaptive learning paths to AI-powered practice environments.

2026 is the new beginning — a new era of continuous development and delivery of educational solutions to help customers, partners, and practitioners succeed with SAP's Cloud and AI strategy.

Together, through collaboration and a relentless commitment to seeking new ways to offer better training using AI, we will ensure that the next generation of SAP BI professionals is equipped for a world where the semantic layer is intelligent, the data is in the cloud, and AI is woven into every decision.

The semantic layer has come a long way from a .UNV file connecting to an Oracle database in 1998. But its core purpose hasn't changed: make data understandable, accessible, and actionable for the people — and now the AI systems — that need it. I'm grateful to have been part of this journey, and I'm energized by what's ahead.

entry.title: Re: BTP account architecture for Joule
entry.content: One Joule subscription can only be integrated with one tenant of an SAP product. System registration and formation creation processes are designed around single global account architecture. All SAP systems (S/4HANA Cloud, SuccessFactors, etc.) must be registered within the same global account for proper system landscape management. Entitlements for Joule services, SAP Build Work Zone standard edition, and Cloud Identity Services must exist within the same global account.

entry.title: DI API SpecialPrices.Update() overwrites SPP1.Price on ALL existing rows
entry.content:
SAP Business One V10 2502 SQL

The DI API SpecialPrices.Update() overwrites SPP1.Price on ALL existing rows with the OSPP.Price header value, regardless of what is set in oDataAreas.SpecialPrice. Only newly added rows have their SpecialPrice written correctly.

The behavior was not observed on UI hen done manually.

see code pasted.

any sugesstions on how to fix is greatly appreciated.

// issuu of SPP1 price reset
SAPbobsCOM.SpecialPrices oSpecialPrices =
    (SAPbobsCOM.SpecialPrices)oCompany.GetBusinessObject(SAPbobsCOM.BoObjectTypes.oSpecialPrices);

oSpecialPrices.CardCode     = "C0001";
oSpecialPrices.ItemCode     = "Item00001";
oSpecialPrices.PriceListNum = 12;
oSpecialPrices.Currency     = "AUD";
oSpecialPrices.Price        = 35.0;  // confirmed current OSPP.Price

SAPbobsCOM.SpecialPricesDataAreas oDataAreas = oSpecialPrices.SpecialPricesDataAreas;

// --- SPP1 Line 0 : existing closed row ---
oDataAreas.SetCurrentLine(0);
oDataAreas.PriceListNo   = 12;
oDataAreas.DateFrom      = new DateTime(2024, 6, 5);
oDataAreas.Dateto        = new DateTime(2026, 3, 23);
oDataAreas.SpecialPrice  = 35.0;
oDataAreas.AutoUpdate    = SAPbobsCOM.BoYesNoEnum.tNO;
oDataAreas.PriceCurrency = "AUD";
SAPbobsCOM.SpecialPricesQuantityAreas oQtyAreas0 = oDataAreas.SpecialPricesQuantityAreas;
oQtyAreas0.Add();
oQtyAreas0.SetCurrentLine(0);
oQtyAreas0.Quantity      = 10.0;
oQtyAreas0.SpecialPrice  = 5.0;
oQtyAreas0.PriceCurrency = "AUD";
oQtyAreas0.Add();
oQtyAreas0.SetCurrentLine(1);
oQtyAreas0.Quantity      = 20.0;
oQtyAreas0.SpecialPrice  = 3.0;
oQtyAreas0.PriceCurrency = "AUD";

// --- SPP1 Line 1 : existing closed row ---

entry.title: SAP Public Cloud 2602 /Authorization Concept for the App List of Supplier Invoices
entry.content:
Hello Experts,
i need help with the following scenario:
The goal is, that a user can access the app List of supplier invoices and that he can view all invoices and attachements, but cant create, edit or cancel anything. The second aspect is, that the user should still be able to recieve workflow tasks for invoices that are Saved as Complete and is able to accept or deny them. 

The issue for in this case is, if i change the custom business role, that i created, and restrict the role to only read access and value help access, the user is no longer able to recieve and work with workflow tasks (as this counts as an edit to the supplier invoice).

My second idea was to create an app variant and remove unwanted buttons. This almost worked but the user is still able to cancel posted invoices, if he uses the app list of supplier invoices. If the user navigates to the invoice from the workflow task, then he enters F7900 and can even post or edit supplier invoices. Based on my tests, im not even able to make UI Adaptions to F7900 and prohibit the user from using these functions.

My question is, how can i proceed from here. Are there any other tools/settings that allow me to restrict the user on this granular level ? Can some restrictiontypes be used for this and not only for different access levels for example by Purchase Organization ? 

Im thankfull for any suggestions.

Best regards
Lukas

entry.title: ISO20022: Format switch from pain.001.001.03 to pain.001.001.09
entry.content:
As part of a migration to ISO 20022 (pain.001.001.09) in SAP (PMW/DMEE), we need to gradually switch existing payments to the new format.

Currently each company code uses about 10 Payment Methods which are actively used in F110.

We have old DMEE tree for pain.001.001.03 and we have a new one for pain.001.001.09.

The challenge is how to perform the switch:

  • Creating new (duplicated) Payment Methods with the new format is not very practical (a lot of configuration, changes in master data, and the Payment Method key is only 1 character).
  • Simply replacing the payment format in an existing Payment Method is risky because it would immediately affect all payments using that method.

What is the usual way to perform a phased and safe rollout of a new payment format in production?

Will be appreciated for ideas or real exampes.

entry.title: element with types abap.dats and abap.dec optional in abstract cds entity
entry.content:
Hi experts,

I have on SAP-HANA an abstract entity for a popup. 

define root abstract entity zi_salesdash_mass_change

@EndUserText.label: 'Popup für Massenänderung'
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
define root abstract entity zi_salesdash_mass_change {
  @EndUserText.label  : 'Versandbedingungen'
  @Consumption.valueHelpDefinition: [{ entity: { name: 'I_ShippingCondition', element: 'ShippingCondition' } }]
  ShippingCondition   : abap.char(2);
  @EndUserText.label  : 'Wunschmenge'
  OrderQuantity       : abap.dec(15,3);
  @EndUserText.label  : 'Lieferdatum'
  DeliveryDate        : abap.dats;
}

All three elements should be optional. But when I press the Confirm button on the popup I get an error message (The property 'OrderQuantity' at offset '86' has an invalid value 'null'.)  for OrderQuantity and DeliveryDate (same value null message for DeliveryDate) when I don't enter a value.

Anyone knows how to solve this problem?

Best regards, Sebastian

entry.title: Direct Debit Mandates with SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity
entry.content:
This blog post explains how SAP Multi-Bank Connectivity (MBC) is used in the direct debit mandate procedures in Contract Accounting in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

You can find more information about the following scenarios with the banks:

  1. Sending Outbound Messages of Mandate Changes:
    When you want to create direct debit mandates or make any mandate changes, you send messages to the bank with the MBC.
  2. Receiving Inbound Messages from Banks:
    After the bank receives your message, you get the messages, such as notification of mandate changes, rejected direct debit instructions, or the cancelled direct debit payments, from the bank through MBC.

You can find more information about the MBC in this blog post Multi Bank Connectivity Overview.

You can learn more about direct debit mandates in the following blog posts

  • How to authorize Direct Debit Payments outside SEPA in Contract Accounting
  • Bank Communication Using Direct Debit Mandate in SAP S4/HANA Public Cloud
  • Notification of Direct Debit Mandate Changes from Banks
  • Synchronization for Direct Debit Mandates
  • Direct Debit Advance Notices

Scenario 1: Outbound Messages (Blue Part of the Diagram)

You send messages relevant to mandate changes or mandate creation to the bank using MBC connection. In this procedure, the messages are sent out to the bank automatically,  especially for more messages that are sent in batches at once, informing about the mandate creation or changes. The involvement of the MBC improves the efficiency.

Prerequisites

 You have maintained the routing settings in the Maintain Routing Settings for Outbound Messages activity (ID: 103501) as follows:

Sender IDReceiver ID Message Type Origin

< SUN number>

Service User Number		GB_BACSGB_AUDDISFICA

These message types are used for the MBC to identify the messages that are sent to the banks (receiver).
Note: You can use the Cloud BAdI BADI_DDM_BKINST_MBC_OUT_PARAMS to change Sender Id, Receiver ID, and Message Type. These values are used in this Maintain Routing Settings for Outbound Messages activity.

Procedure

  1. You have created a direct debit mandate or a made a mandate change.
  2. Open the Create Direct Debit Mandate Instructions Media app (F7520). When you schedule a run, select the Send Message (MBC) checkbox.
  3. To see the processed messages, go to the Manage Bank Messages app (F4385).
    Note: Ensure that you have the same minimum authorization as delivered with the Business Integration Specialist - Bank integration (SAP_BR_BANK_INT_SPECIALIST) business role template.
    Select the message types that you have configured.

    See the messages that have been sent to the bank.
  4. You can view or download the files in the Manage Direct Debit Mandate Media app.

Scenario 2: Inbound Messages (Red Part of the Diagram)

After you sent the outbound messages to the bank, you may receive messages back as follows:

  • Notification of mandate changes if the mandate changes are applied
  • Rejected direct debit instructions if the mandate changes are rejected

After you execute the payments with direct debit, you may receive from the bank messages about cancelled direct debit payments.

Prerequisites

  1. You have created a direct debit mandate or a made a mandate change.
  2. You have maintained the routing settings in the Maintain Processing Steps for Inbound Messages activity (ID: 103504) as follows:

    Sender ID

    Receiver ID

    Message Type

    Application

    <House bank ID>

    <Your ID>

    GB_ADDACS

    DDM_NOC

    <House bank ID>

    <Your ID>

    GB_AUDDIS_REJ

    DDM_NOC

    <House bank ID>

    <Your ID>

    GB_ARUDD

    DDM_RJCT


    Note: Messages delivered through the MBC carries the following types of files:

    • Changes to direct debit mandates or rejections to mandate changes (application type DDM_NOC)
    • Cancelled direct debit payments (application type DDM_RJCT)

    Upon receiving the messages from the banks through MBC, the messages are categorized based on the application type and proceed with different actions accordingly.

    • For the files that are assigned with the DDM_NOC application type, the system automatically imports the files in the Import Direct Debit Mandate Instructions Media app.
    • For the files that are assigned with the DDM_RJCT application type, the system automatically creates jobs in the Manage Incoming Payment Files app.

In the following diagram, you can see how the MBC automatically processes files from the banks based on the application types. After the automatic processing, you can view the processed messages in the Manage Bank Messages app.
Note: To access this app, you have the same minimum authorization as delivered with the Business Integration Specialist - Bank Integration (SAP_BR_BANK_INT_SPECIALIST) business role template.

3. You have maintained the statement parameter set assignments in the Maintain Processing Steps for Inbound Messages (ID: 103504) activity.

Optionally, you can implement your own logic by using the Business Add-In (BAdI) BADI_DDM_NOC_MBC_IMPORT_PARAMS to identify the messages sent by banks and convert them into the Import Direct Debit Mandate Instructions Media app.

Procedure

You can directly use the Manage Bank Messages app (F4385) to view messages as follows:

Select the message you’d like to view.

Select the file to download and view the XML file.

Is this blog post useful to you? Leave your comments and let us know your thoughts?

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA vs C4C as Leading System for Business Partner...
entry.content:
¡Hola! 
@juanfag1018.

Voy a escribir en tu lengua materna, pido perdón si hay algún error gramatical.

Hoy, cuando hablamos de CRM, SAP C4C es un CRM. Contamos con tres enfoques de integración: S/4HANA → C4C (replicación), C4C → S/4HANA y el enfoque bidireccional. En muchos proyectos se adopta el enfoque bidireccional, con el objetivo de centralizar los datos en SAP S/4HANA (ERP). Sin embargo, el proceso suele comenzar en SAP C4C mediante la función BUP002 (cliente potencial), conocida como “pre-registro”, seguida de la integración con S/4HANA. También existe la posibilidad de crear el cliente directamente en S/4HANA y replicarlo hacia SAP C4C, lo que aporta mayor flexibilidad en el uso del CRM.

Un punto importante de atención: No se recomienda replicar todos los registros de clientes hacia SAP S/4HANA, sino únicamente aquellos con potencial real de venta. En este contexto, se puede utilizar una integración selectiva basada en clientes con la función CRM000.

De esta manera, se garantiza una gobernanza adecuada de los IDs de cliente tanto en SAP C4C como en SAP S/4HANA, evitando duplicidades u otros problemas de consistencia de datos.

Si tiene alguna duda en el texto o hay algún punto que no haya podido explicar con precisión, por favor, pregunte.

Gracias,

Matheus Paschoal Raphael

entry.title: Re: SAP Public Cloud 2602 /Authorization Concept for the...
entry.content:
Hello @LukasKuhn,

I think using the concept of IAM Apps can help you here to proceed with your second idea “to create an app variant and remove unwanted buttons. This almost worked but the user is still able to cancel posted invoices, if he uses the app list of supplier invoices. If the user navigates to the invoice from the workflow task, then he enters F7900 and can even post or edit supplier invoices.”

 

The App F1060A Supplier Invoices List is part of two Business Catalogs.

 

Both of those Business Catalogs support IAM apps.

 

This means that you can activate and de-activate single apps from this catalog in each role.

 

To do so:

  • Open the relevant role in the App Maintain Business Roles.
  • In Edit Mode, you can deactivate the relevant app(s).

 

After the deactivation, the user will not be able to navigate to the app Supplier Invoices List and therefore will not be able to edit supplier invoices.

You could then proceed with your second idea via an app variant.

 

However, there are two caveats to this idea:

  1. The app can no longer be opened at all (not even to display invoices).
  2. There are other apps, such as Create Supplier Invoice that may also need to be disabled.

 

I hope this helps.

 

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: Re: Announcing: Enhancements to Quality Gates and Embedded Launch Activities for HCM and SAP Cloud E
entry.content:
A recurring pain point for System Integrators, Professional Services teams, and Customers is ensuring that the productive SuccessFactors configuration remains fully aligned with the signed‑off testing scope and outcomes at each Quality Gate (Q‑Gate). This challenge is amplified by the reliance on configuration workbooks as the primary reference and backup documentation for Q‑Gate approvals, which often diverge over time from what is actually configured in the system.

SAP should propose exploring an automated capability that can generate an authoritative, system-driven “configuration blueprint” from the finalized SuccessFactors tenant, analogous in spirit to the Business Blueprint produced in the classic ASAP methodology, where approved process design becomes the basis for build and testing. In this model, once a test phase is signed off at a Q‑Gate, the underlying configuration is captured and stamped as a baseline, and the corresponding blueprint/workbook is regenerated directly from the system to serve as the single source of truth for subsequent phases and audits.

Expected Benefits: 

  • Faster adoption: Reduces manual documentation effort, accelerates handover from project to operations, and shortens time to value for customers and partners.
  • Higher quality and compliance: Strengthens the linkage between Q‑Gate criteria, approved test results, and actual system settings, improving auditability and reducing go‑live risk.
  • Reusability: Enables reusable, system-derived blueprints and templates that can be leveraged across rollouts, countries, and future phases.
  • Cost savings: Lowers project and run costs for customers, System Integrators, and SAP by minimizing rework, misalignment between documents and system, and inefficient manual updates to configuration workbook
